restriction_notes: Bicycles enter freely. No special paperwork beyond your passport and visa. Customs may briefly inspect panniers.
documents_notes: That's it! Bicycles are the simplest vehicle to bring into Iran. No carnet, no insurance, no plates required.
road_quality_notes: Main highways have shoulders suitable for cycling. Secondary roads vary — some excellent, some rough. Desert sections can have sand on road edges. Mountain passes have good surfaces but steep grades. Avoid cycling in major cities (Tehran, Isfahan, Mashhad) — traffic is chaotic.
repair_availability: Basic bicycle repair shops in most towns. High-quality parts (Shimano, etc.) mainly available in Tehran. Carry essential spares: tubes, brake pads, chain links, cables. Iranian mechanics are resourceful and will improvise fixes.
spare_parts_notes: Tehran: Iran Mall area has good bike shops. Isfahan: shops near Naqsh-e Jahan. Shiraz: limited. Carry your own touring-specific parts.
breakdown_assistance: Flag someone down — Iranians will go out of their way to help a stranded cyclist. Many will insist on driving you to the nearest town.
guide_requirement_notes: Since Sep 2025, travelers from EU/US/UK/CA/AU/NZ need licensed guide for entire trip — this applies to cyclists too. The guide doesn't need to ride with you but must be coordinating your route and accommodations.

restriction_notes: Under 250cc motorcycles are fully legal. No issues at any border.
insurance_notes: Third-party insurance can be purchased at the border or in the nearest city. ~$30 for 30 days.
fuel_notes: Gasoline ~$0.08/liter (subsidized). Tourists can buy at pumps without Iranian fuel card. Some stations may charge slightly more (~$0.15). Stations in all cities and every 50-80km on highways.
carnet_issuing_body: TACI - Touring & Automobile Club of Iran
carnet_deposit_notes: Iran classified as AIT/FIA Band 7 (high). Deposit can be significant. Check with your national automobile association (ADAC, RAC/CARS UK, AAA AU, TCS, etc.).
triptych_notes: Triptych booklet available from TACI for single-country entry. Up to 10 entries. Alternative to full CDP.
road_quality_notes: Iranian main roads are well-maintained. Highway surfaces are good. Watch for potholes on secondary roads. Gravel sections in remote areas. Desert sand encroachment on some roads in Sistan-Baluchestan and south Khorasan.
repair_availability: Iranian mechanics are legendary for motorcycle repairs. Small Honda/Yamaha parts widely available (Iran produces Honda CG125 clones). Foreign brand parts harder to find — carry essentials.
guide_requirement_notes: Since Sep 2025: guide requirement applies. Guide coordinates your itinerary but doesn't need to ride pillion.

restriction_notes: TECHNICALLY restricted: Iranian law limits >250cc motorcycles to 5-day stay. IN PRACTICE: ~95% of foreign tourists on big bikes enter without issue and stay the full visa duration. Border officials understand these are touring bikes, not permanent imports. Keep documents ready, be polite, and expect to spend extra time at customs. US-made motorcycles (Harley-Davidson, Indian) are explicitly banned.
banned_notes: US-made motorcycles are prohibited from entry. All other brands (BMW, KTM, Honda, Yamaha, Triumph, Ducati, etc.) are fine in practice.
repair_availability: BMW GS, KTM, Honda Africa Twin parts NOT available locally. Carry: oil filters, brake pads, levers, inner tubes (if applicable), chain and sprockets, spark plugs. Iranian mechanics can fabricate many parts. Tehran has a few shops familiar with large adventure bikes.
guide_requirement_notes: Guide requirement applies per Sep 2025 rules.

restriction_notes: Since April 2024, vehicles with international plates no longer need temporary Iranian plates. Previously a major friction point — now resolved. Luxury vehicles (Porsche, Lamborghini, Ferrari, Maserati, Bugatti) and all US-made cars are banned.
banned_notes: US-made vehicles and luxury/high-end vehicles are prohibited. Since 2019, the $40,000 value cap and 2500cc engine limit have been lifted for most vehicles.
insurance_notes: Mandatory third-party. ~$50 for 30 days. Can purchase at border or first major city. Comprehensive coverage available from Iranian insurers.
road_quality_notes: Iran has excellent highway infrastructure. Multi-lane expressways connect major cities. Tehran–Isfahan, Tehran–Mashhad, Isfahan–Shiraz are all high-quality roads. Mountain passes in Zagros and Alborz are scenic but sometimes steep/winding. Remote desert roads in Sistan-Baluchestan can be rough.
police_checkpoint_notes: Frequent police checkpoints throughout Iran. Always have passport, visa, carnet, registration, and insurance accessible. Officers are generally friendly and curious about foreign vehicles. Some may want a selfie with your car.
repair_availability: Mechanics everywhere. Iran produces Peugeot 206/405 and SAIPA cars domestically — parts for these are everywhere. Parts for other European/Japanese cars available in major cities. Specialist 4×4 shops in Tehran.
guide_requirement_notes: Guide requirement applies per Sep 2025 rules.

restriction_notes: Campervans and motorhomes are welcome. Wild camping is legal and widely practiced. Since 2019, engine size and value restrictions have been lifted. No US-made vehicles.
fuel_notes: Diesel also available at subsidized rates. LPG refills possible in major cities.
road_quality_notes: Most highways accommodate campervans. Some mountain roads have tight switchbacks. Watch height restrictions on older bridges. Tehran traffic is brutal in large vehicles — avoid. Parking in city centers can be challenging.
repair_availability: Sprinter/Mercedes vans well-known in Iran. Parts available. Specialist campervan services in Tehran. For other chassis, carry spares.
